sourceQuestion:
Let Sigma and Delta be finite alphabets and let # be a symbol outside both Sigma and Delta. Let f be a total function from Sigma* to Delta*. We say f is computable if there exists a Turing machine which given an input w in Sigma*, always halts with f(w) on its tape. Let L_f denote the language { w#f(w) | w in Sigma* }. Which of the following statements is true?

A. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable)
B. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursively enumerable
C. If f is computable then L_f is recursive, but not conversely
D. If L_f is recursively enumerable then f is computable, but not conversely
sourceAnswer: A. f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable)

parsed.stem: Let Sigma and Delta be finite alphabets and let # be a symbol outside both Sigma and Delta. Let f be a total function from Sigma* to Delta*. We say f is computable if there exists a Turing machine which given an input w in Sigma*, always halts with f(w) on its tape. Let L_f denote the language { w#f(w) | w in Sigma* }. Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: f is computable if and only if L_f is recursive (decidable)
f is computable if and only if L_f is recursively enumerable
If f is computable then L_f is recursive, but not conversely
If L_f is recursively enumerable then f is computable, but not conversely

sourceQuestion:
Let L(R) be the language represented by regular expression R. Let L(G) be the language generated by a context free grammar G. Let L(M) be the language accepted by a Turing machine M. Which of the following decision problems are undecidable?
I. Given a regular expression R and a string w, is w in L(R)?
II. Given a context-free grammar G, is L(G) = Sigma* for some alphabet Sigma?
III. Given a Turing machine M and a string w, is w in L(M)?

A. I and II only
B. III only
C. II and III only
D. III and IV only (I, II and III)

parsed.stem:
Let L(R) be the language represented by regular expression R. Let L(G) be the language generated by a context free grammar G. Let L(M) be the language accepted by a Turing machine M. Which of the following decision problems are undecidable?
I. Given a regular expression R and a string w, is w in L(R)?
II. Given a context-free grammar G, is L(G) = Sigma* for some alphabet Sigma?
III. Given a Turing machine M and a string w, is w in L(M)?

options.text: III and IV only (I, II and III)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following problems. L(G) denotes the language generated by a grammar G. L(M) denotes the language accepted by a machine M.
I. For an unrestricted grammar G and a string w, whether w in L(G)
II. Given a Turing machine M, whether L(M) is regular
III. Given two context-free grammars G_1 and G_2, whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
IV. Given an NFA N, whether there is a deterministic PDA P such that L(N) = L(P)

Which of the following is correct?

A. Only I and II are undecidable
B. Only II is undecidable
C. Only I, II and III are undecidable
D. Only I and III are undecidable
sourceAnswer: C. Only I, II and III are undecidable

parsed.stem:
Consider the following problems. L(G) denotes the language generated by a grammar G. L(M) denotes the language accepted by a machine M.
I. For an unrestricted grammar G and a string w, whether w in L(G)
II. Given a Turing machine M, whether L(M) is regular
III. Given two context-free grammars G_1 and G_2, whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
IV. Given an NFA N, whether there is a deterministic PDA P such that L(N) = L(P)

Which of the following is correct?

options.text: Only I and II are undecidable
Only I, II and III are undecidable
Only I and III are undecidable

sourceAnswer: C. L_1, L_3, and L_4 only

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two statements about regular languages:
S_1: Every infinite regular language contains an undecidable language as a subset.
S_2: Every finite language is regular.

Which one of the following choices is correct?
A. Only S_1 is true
B. Only S_2 is true
C. Both S_1 and S_2 are true
D. Neither S_1 nor S_2 is true
sourceAnswer: C. Both S_1 and S_2 are true

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two statements about regular languages:
S_1: Every infinite regular language contains an undecidable language as a subset.
S_2: Every finite language is regular.

Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.text: Both S_1 and S_2 are true
Neither S_1 nor S_2 is true

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is/are undecidable?

A. Given two Turing machines M_1 and M_2, decide if L(M_1) = L(M_2).
B. Given a Turing machine M, decide if L(M) is regular.
C. Given a Turing machine M, decide if M takes more than 1073 steps on every string.
D. Given a Turing machine M, decide if M accepts all strings.

parsed.stem: Which of the following is/are undecidable?

options.text: Given two Turing machines M_1 and M_2, decide if L(M_1) = L(M_2).
Given a Turing machine M, decide if L(M) is regular.
Given a Turing machine M, decide if M takes more than 1073 steps on every string.
Given a Turing machine M, decide if M accepts all strings.

sourceQuestion:
Which ONE of the following languages is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA)?

A. Any regular language
B. Any context-free language
C. Any language accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton
D. Any decidable language

parsed.stem: Which ONE of the following languages is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA)?

options.text: Any language accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following set can be recognized by a Deterministic Finite state Automaton?
A. The numbers 1, 2, 4, 8, 2^1, ... written in binary
B. The numbers 1, 2, 4, 8, 2^1, ... written in unary
C. The set of binary strings in which the number of zeros is the same as the number of ones.
D. The set {1, 101, 11011, 1110111, ...}

parsed.stem: Which of the following set can be recognized by a Deterministic Finite state Automaton?

options.text: The set of binary strings in which the number of zeros is the same as the number of ones.

sourceQuestion:
The finite state machine described by the following state diagram with A as starting state, where an arc label is x/y and x stands for 1-bit input and y stands for 1-bit output:
(State diagram: Start->A, A--0/0-->A, A--1/0-->B, B--0/1-->B, B--1/0-->A, with self-loops and cross transitions as shown)
A. outputs the sum of the present and the previous bits of the input
B. outputs 1 whenever the input sequence contains 11
C. outputs 1 whenever the input sequence contains 10
D. none of the above
sourceAnswer: C. outputs 1 whenever the input sequence contains 10

parsed.stem:
The finite state machine described by the following state diagram with A as starting state, where an arc label is x/y and x stands for 1-bit input and y stands for 1-bit output:
(State diagram: Start->A, A--0/0-->A, A--1/0-->B, B--0/1-->B, B--1/0-->A, with self-loops and cross transitions as shown)

options.text: outputs the sum of the present and the previous bits of the input
outputs 1 whenever the input sequence contains 11
outputs 1 whenever the input sequence contains 10

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following deterministic finite state automaton M.
[DFA M has 3 states: start state q0, state q1, state q2 (accepting). Transitions: q0 on 0 -> q0, q0 on 1 -> q1; q1 on 0 -> q2, q1 on 1 -> q0; q2 on 0 -> q1, q2 on 1 -> q2.]
Let S denote the set of seven-bit binary strings in which the first, the fourth, and the last bits are 1. The number of strings in S that are accepted by M is:
A. 1
B. 5
C. 7
D. 8

parsed.stem:
Consider the following deterministic finite state automaton M.
[DFA M has 3 states: start state q0, state q1, state q2 (accepting). Transitions: q0 on 0 -> q0, q0 on 1 -> q1; q1 on 0 -> q2, q1 on 1 -> q0; q2 on 0 -> q1, q2 on 1 -> q2.]
Let S denote the set of seven-bit binary strings in which the first, the fourth, and the last bits are 1. The number of strings in S that are accepted by M is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the NFA M shown below.
[NFA M has states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accepting). Transitions: q0 on 0 -> {q0,q1}, q0 on 1 -> {q0}; q1 on 0 -> {q2}, q1 on 1 -> {q2}; q2 on 0 -> {q3}, q2 on 1 -> {q3}; q3 has no transitions.]
Let the language accepted by M be L. Let L_2 be the language accepted by the NFA M_2 obtained by changing the accepting state of M to a non-accepting state and by changing the non-accepting states of M to accepting states. Which of the following statements is true?
A. L_2 = {0,1}* - L
B. L_2 = {0,1}*
C. L_1 subset_of L_2
D. L_1 = L_2

parsed.stem:
Consider the NFA M shown below.
[NFA M has states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accepting). Transitions: q0 on 0 -> {q0,q1}, q0 on 1 -> {q0}; q1 on 0 -> {q2}, q1 on 1 -> {q2}; q2 on 0 -> {q3}, q2 on 1 -> {q3}; q3 has no transitions.]
Let the language accepted by M be L. Let L_2 be the language accepted by the NFA M_2 obtained by changing the accepting state of M to a non-accepting state and by changing the non-accepting states of M to accepting states. Which of the following statements is true?

sourceQuestion:
The following finite state machine accepts all those binary strings in which the number of 1's and 0's are respectively:
[FSM takes binary input from the least significant bit. The machine has 4 states arranged to track parity. Start state is labeled 'start'. States track even/odd counts of 1s and 0s.]
A. divisible by 3 and 2
B. odd and even
C. even and odd
D. divisible by 2 and 3

parsed.stem:
The following finite state machine accepts all those binary strings in which the number of 1's and 0's are respectively:
[FSM takes binary input from the least significant bit. The machine has 4 states arranged to track parity. Start state is labeled 'start'. States track even/odd counts of 1s and 0s.]

sourceAnswer: B. {w ∈ {a,b}* | every a in w is followed by at least two b's}

parsed.stem:
Consider the machine M:

[NFA with states q0 (start), q1, q2 over alphabet {a, b}. From q0: on 'a' go to q1, on 'b' stay at q0. From q1: on 'b' go to q2, on 'a' go to q1. From q2: on 'b' go to q0 (accept), on 'a' go to q1. q0 is also accepting.]

The language recognized by M is:

options.text: {w ∈ {a,b}* | every a in w is followed by exactly two b's}
{w ∈ {a,b}* | every a in w is followed by at least two b's}
{w ∈ {a,b}* | w does not contain 'aa' as a substring}

sourceQuestion:
The following diagram represents a finite state machine which takes as input a binary number from the least significant bit.

[FSM with two states: Start state S0 and state S1. Transitions: S0 on input 0 -> S0 (output 0), S0 on input 1 -> S1 (output 1), S1 on input 0 -> S1 (output 1), S1 on input 1 -> S1 (output 0).]

Which of the following is TRUE?

A. It computes 1's complement of the input number
B. It computes 2's complement of the input number
C. It increments the input number
D. It decrements the input number
sourceAnswer: B. It computes 2's complement of the input number

parsed.stem:
The following diagram represents a finite state machine which takes as input a binary number from the least significant bit.

[FSM with two states: Start state S0 and state S1. Transitions: S0 on input 0 -> S0 (output 0), S0 on input 1 -> S1 (output 1), S1 on input 0 -> S1 (output 1), S1 on input 1 -> S1 (output 0).]

Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: It computes 1's complement of the input number
It computes 2's complement of the input number

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following Finite State Automaton:

[FSA with states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3. Transitions: q0 --b--> q0 (self-loop), q0 --a--> q1, q1 --a--> q1 (self-loop), q1 --b--> q2, q2 --a--> q1, q2 --b--> q0, q3 is the accepting state reachable via the alternating ba path from q0.]

The language accepted by this automaton is given by the regular expression

A. b*ab*
B. (a + b)*
C. b*(a(a + b)*)
D. b*a(ba)*b*

parsed.stem:
Consider the following Finite State Automaton:

[FSA with states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3. Transitions: q0 --b--> q0 (self-loop), q0 --a--> q1, q1 --a--> q1 (self-loop), q1 --b--> q2, q2 --a--> q1, q2 --b--> q0, q3 is the accepting state reachable via the alternating ba path from q0.]

The language accepted by this automaton is given by the regular expression

sourceQuestion:
Given below are two finite state automata (-> indicates the start state and F indicates a final state):

Y:
     a   b
->1  1   2
 2(F) 1   1

Z:
     a   b
->1  2   2
 2(F) 2   1

Which of the following represents the product automaton Z x Y?

A.
     a   b
->P  S   R
  Q  R   S
  R  Q   P
  S  P   Q

B.
     a   b
->P   S   R
  Q(F) R   S
  R(F) Q   P
  S    P   Q

C.
     a   b
->P   Q   R
  Q   R   S
  R(F) Q   P
  S    P   Q

D.
     a   b
->P   S   Q
  Q   R   S
  R(F) Q   P
  S    P   Q
sourceAnswer: B. Start P, finals Q and R, transitions: P->S(a), P->R(b), Q->R(a), Q->S(b), R->Q(a), R->P(b), S->P(a), S->Q(b)

parsed.stem:
Given below are two finite state automata (-> indicates the start state and F indicates a final state):

Y:
     a   b
->1  1   2
 2(F) 1   1

Z:
     a   b
->1  2   2
 2(F) 2   1

Which of the following represents the product automaton Z x Y?

options.text: a b ->P S R Q R S R Q P S P Q
a b ->P S R Q(F) R S R(F) Q P S P Q
a b ->P Q R Q R S R(F) Q P S P Q
a b ->P S Q Q R S R(F) Q P S P Q

sourceQuestion:
Match the following NFAs with the regular expressions they correspond to:

P: [NFA - start state is also accepting state; from start reading 0 goes to an intermediate state; from intermediate state reading 1 or 00 loops back; reading 1 returns to start/accept]

Q: [NFA - similar structure but with additional 0* transitions in the loop, making it more complex]

Regular expressions:
1. epsilon + 0(1 + 00)*1
2. epsilon + 0(1 + 00)*0
3. 0(1 + 00)*1
4. epsilon + 0(10*1 + 0)*1

A. P - 1, Q - 4
B. P - 1, Q - 3
C. P - 4, Q - 3
D. P - 1, Q - 2

parsed.stem:
Match the following NFAs with the regular expressions they correspond to:

P: [NFA - start state is also accepting state; from start reading 0 goes to an intermediate state; from intermediate state reading 1 or 00 loops back; reading 1 returns to start/accept]

Q: [NFA - similar structure but with additional 0* transitions in the loop, making it more complex]

Regular expressions:
1. epsilon + 0(1 + 00)*1
2. epsilon + 0(1 + 00)*0
3. 0(1 + 00)*1
4. epsilon + 0(10*1 + 0)*1

sourceQuestion:
The above DFA accepts the set of all strings over {0, 1} that:

A. begin with 0 or 1
B. end with 0
C. end with 1
D. contain the substring 00

sourceQuestion:
What is the complement of the language accepted by the NFA shown below?
Assume Sigma = {a} and epsilon is the empty string.

(The NFA has a start state with an epsilon-transition to an accepting state, and a self-loop on 'a' at the start state.)

A. phi
B. {epsilon}
C. a*
D. {a, epsilon}

parsed.stem:
What is the complement of the language accepted by the NFA shown below?
Assume Sigma = {a} and epsilon is the empty string.

(The NFA has a start state with an epsilon-transition to an accepting state, and a self-loop on 'a' at the start state.)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the set of strings on {0,1} in which, every substring of 3 symbols has at most two 0s. For example, 001, 010, 011 are in the language, but 000 is not. All strings of length less than 3 are also in the language. A partially completed DFA that accepts this language is shown below.

(The DFA has states: epsilon (start), 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, and q (dead/trap state). States 00 and 01 and 10 and 11 represent the last two symbols read.)

The missing arcs in the DFA are:

A.
  State 00 on 0 -> 1, State 00 on nothing -> (blank), State 01 on 1 -> 1, State 10 on 0 -> 0, State 11 on 0 -> (blank)
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-00=1,col-01=0; 01-row: col-11=1; 10-row: col-00=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

B.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=0,col-q=1; 01-row: col-01=1; 10-row: col-11=0; 11-row: col-01=0)

C.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=1,col-q=0; 01-row: col-01=1; 10-row: col-10=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

D.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=1,col-q=0; 01-row: col-11=1; 10-row: col-00=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

parsed.stem:
Consider the set of strings on {0,1} in which, every substring of 3 symbols has at most two 0s. For example, 001, 010, 011 are in the language, but 000 is not. All strings of length less than 3 are also in the language. A partially completed DFA that accepts this language is shown below.

(The DFA has states: epsilon (start), 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, and q (dead/trap state). States 00 and 01 and 10 and 11 represent the last two symbols read.)

The missing arcs in the DFA are:

options.text: State 00 on 0 -> 1, State 00 on nothing -> (blank), State 01 on 1 -> 1, State 10 on 0 -> 0, State 11 on 0 -> (blank) (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-00=1,col-01=0; 01-row: col-11=1; 10-row: col-00=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

parsed.stem:
Consider the finite automaton in the following figure:

[NFA over {0,1}: States q0 (start), q1, q2, q3. Transitions: q0 --0--> q0, q0 --0--> q1, q0 --1--> q0; q1 --0.1--> q1 (self-loop on 0 and 1), q1 --0.1--> q2; q2 is accept state with transitions; final structure accepts strings containing '0011' as a substring (NFA with epsilon transitions leads to reachable state set)]

What is the set of reachable states for the input string 0011?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two statements:
I. If all states of an NFA are accepting states then the language accepted by the NFA is Sigma*.
II. There exists a regular language A such that for all languages B, A ∩ B is regular.

Which one of the following is CORRECT?
A. Only I is true
B. Only II is true
C. Both I and II are true
D. Both I and II are false

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two statements:
I. If all states of an NFA are accepting states then the language accepted by the NFA is Sigma*.
II. There exists a regular language A such that for all languages B, A ∩ B is regular.

Which one of the following is CORRECT?

options.text: Both I and II are true
Both I and II are false

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we want to design a synchronous circuit that processes a string of 0's and 1's. Given a string, it produces another string by replacing the first 1 in any subsequence of consecutive 1's by a 0. Consider the following example.

Input sequence:  0010001000111001100
Output sequence: 0000001000011001100

A Mealy Machine is a state machine where both the next state and the output are functions of the present state and the current input.

The states in the Mealy machine can be represented using Boolean variables b and a. We denote the current state, the next state, the next incoming bit, and the output bit by the variables a, b, x, t, and y respectively.

Assume the initial state of the machine is 0.

What are the Boolean expressions corresponding to t and y in terms of a, b and x?

A. t = a + b,  y = ab'x'
B. t = b,  y = ab
C. t = a + b,  y = abx'
D. t = ab',  y = ab
sourceAnswer: A. t = a + b,  y = ab'x'

parsed.stem:
Suppose we want to design a synchronous circuit that processes a string of 0's and 1's. Given a string, it produces another string by replacing the first 1 in any subsequence of consecutive 1's by a 0. Consider the following example.

Input sequence:  0010001000111001100
Output sequence: 0000001000011001100

A Mealy Machine is a state machine where both the next state and the output are functions of the present state and the current input.

The states in the Mealy machine can be represented using Boolean variables b and a. We denote the current state, the next state, the next incoming bit, and the output bit by the variables a, b, x, t, and y respectively.

Assume the initial state of the machine is 0.

What are the Boolean expressions corresponding to t and y in terms of a, b and x?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the 5-state DFA M accepting the language L(M) shown below.

The DFA has 5 states: 1 (start), 2, 3, 4, 5 (accepting states: 2 and 4).
Transitions:
  From 1: on 0 -> 2, on 1 -> 3
  From 2: on 0 -> 4, on 1 -> 5
  From 3: on 0 -> 5, on 1 -> 4
  From 4: on 0 -> 2, on 1 -> 3
  From 5: on 0 -> 5, on 1 -> 5

For any string w in (0+1)*, let n_0(w) be the number of 0's in w and n_1(w) be the number of 1's in w.

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

A. States 2 and 4 are distinguishable in M
B. States 3 and 5 are distinguishable in M
C. States 2 and 5 are distinguishable in M
D. Any string w with n_0(w) = n_1(w) is in L(M)
sourceAnswer: D. Any string w with n_0(w) = n_1(w) is in L(M)

parsed.stem:
Consider the 5-state DFA M accepting the language L(M) shown below.

The DFA has 5 states: 1 (start), 2, 3, 4, 5 (accepting states: 2 and 4).
Transitions:
  From 1: on 0 -> 2, on 1 -> 3
  From 2: on 0 -> 4, on 1 -> 5
  From 3: on 0 -> 5, on 1 -> 4
  From 4: on 0 -> 2, on 1 -> 3
  From 5: on 0 -> 5, on 1 -> 5

For any string w in (0+1)*, let n_0(w) be the number of 0's in w and n_1(w) be the number of 1's in w.

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

options.text: States 2 and 4 are distinguishable in M
States 3 and 5 are distinguishable in M
States 2 and 5 are distinguishable in M
Any string w with n_0(w) = n_1(w) is in L(M)

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following regular expressions is equivalent to the language accepted by the DFA given below?

[DFA over alphabet {0,1}: States q0 (start, final), q1, q2, q3. Transitions: q0 --0--> q0, q0 --1--> q1, q1 --1--> q1, q1 --0--> q2, q2 --0--> q0, q2 --1--> q3, q3 --0--> q3, q3 --1--> q3. Only q0 is an accepting state.]

A. 0*(10 + 10*)1*0*
B. 0*(10)*1*0*
C. 0*1010*1*0*
D. 0(1 + 0*1*)0*

parsed.stem:
Which one of the following regular expressions is equivalent to the language accepted by the DFA given below?

[DFA over alphabet {0,1}: States q0 (start, final), q1, q2, q3. Transitions: q0 --0--> q0, q0 --1--> q1, q1 --1--> q1, q1 --0--> q2, q2 --0--> q0, q2 --1--> q3, q3 --0--> q3, q3 --1--> q3. Only q0 is an accepting state.]

sourceQuestion:
Let M be the 5-state NFA with epsilon-transitions shown in the diagram below.

[5-state NFA M over {0,1}: States 1 (start), 2, 3, 4, 5. Transitions: 1 --eps--> 2, 1 --eps--> 4, 2 --0--> 2, 2 --0--> 3, 3 --eps--> 2, 4 --1--> 4, 4 --1--> 5, 5 --eps--> 4. States 3 and 5 are accepting states.]

Which one of the following regular expressions represents the language accepted by M?

A. (00)* + (11)*
B. 0* + (1 + (00)*(11)*)*
C. (00)* + (1 + (00)*(11)*)*
D. 0* + (1 + (00)*(11))* = 0(11)*

parsed.stem:
Let M be the 5-state NFA with epsilon-transitions shown in the diagram below.

[5-state NFA M over {0,1}: States 1 (start), 2, 3, 4, 5. Transitions: 1 --eps--> 2, 1 --eps--> 4, 2 --0--> 2, 2 --0--> 3, 3 --eps--> 2, 4 --1--> 4, 4 --1--> 5, 5 --eps--> 4. States 3 and 5 are accepting states.]

Which one of the following regular expressions represents the language accepted by M?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following deterministic finite automaton (DFA) defined over the alphabet Sigma = {a, b}. The DFA has 4 states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accept). Transitions: q0 --a--> q1, q0 --b--> q0; q1 --a--> q1, q1 --b--> q2; q2 --a--> q1, q2 --b--> q3; q3 --a--> q3, q3 --b--> q3 (q3 is a dead/trap state for some interpretations). Looking at the DFA image: start state with self-loop on b, transitions on a lead forward, and the accept state is reached after seeing pattern 'abb'.

Identify which of the following language(s) is/are accepted by the given DFA.

A. The set of all strings containing an even number of b's.
B. The set of all strings containing the pattern abb.
C. The set of all strings not starting with a.
D. The set of all strings containing the pattern aba.

parsed.stem:
Consider the following deterministic finite automaton (DFA) defined over the alphabet Sigma = {a, b}. The DFA has 4 states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accept). Transitions: q0 --a--> q1, q0 --b--> q0; q1 --a--> q1, q1 --b--> q2; q2 --a--> q1, q2 --b--> q3; q3 --a--> q3, q3 --b--> q3 (q3 is a dead/trap state for some interpretations). Looking at the DFA image: start state with self-loop on b, transitions on a lead forward, and the accept state is reached after seeing pattern 'abb'.

Identify which of the following language(s) is/are accepted by the given DFA.

options.text: The set of all strings containing an even number of b's.
The set of all strings containing the pattern abb.
The set of all strings not starting with a.
The set of all strings containing the pattern aba.

sourceQuestion:
Let M = (K, Sigma, pi, A, F) be a finite state automaton, where K = {A, B}, Sigma = {a, b}, A = A (start state), F = {B} (accept states).

A grammar to generate the language accepted by M can be specified as G = (V, Sigma, R, S), where V = K union Sigma, and S = A.

Transitions: pi(A, a) = B, pi(B, a) = B, pi(B, b) = B (the FSA accepts a^+ (a followed by any sequence of a's and b's), i.e., strings starting with 'a').

Which one of the following set of rules will make L(G) = L(M)?

A. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon
B. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> a, B -> b
C. A -> aB, A -> a, B -> aB, B -> bB
D. A -> aA, A -> b, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon
sourceAnswer: A. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon

sourceQuestion:
Consider the non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA) shown in the figure. The NFA has states {q0, q1, q2} over alphabet {0, 1}. State q0 is the start state and q2 is the only accepting state. Transitions include epsilon moves.

Let language L1 = L2 denote the language accepted by the NFA with state Z as the only accepting state. Let language L2 denote the language accepted by the NFA with state Z as the only accepting state. The question states: State X is the starting state of the automaton. Let the language accepted by the NFA with Z as the only accepting state be L2. Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. L1 = L2
B. L1 subset L2
C. L2 subset L1
D. None of the above
